perturbation.original_text: 1. California Business and Professions Code Sections 20000 through 20043 provide rights to you concerning termination or non-renewal of a franchise. If the Master Franchise Agreement contains provisions that are inconsistent with the law, the law will control.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. California Business and Professions Code Sections 20000 through 20043 provide rights to you concerning termination or non-renewal of a franchise. Regardless of whether the Master Franchise Agreement contains provisions that are inconsistent with the law, the Master Franchise Agreement will always control.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that 'the Master Franchise Agreement will always control' regardless of inconsistencies with California law, this contradicts the original statement that 'the law will control' when inconsistencies exist. This creates uncertainty about which set of rules governs franchise termination and non-renewal, and gives the agreement precedence over state law. The contradictory text is within the same section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. California Business and Professions Code Sections 20000 through 20043 provide rights to you concerning termination or non-renewal of a franchise. Regardless of whether the Master Franchise Agreement contains provisions that are inconsistent with the law, the Master Franchise Agreement will always control.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the Master Franchise Agreement will always control, regardless of inconsistencies with California law (Business and Professions Code Sections 20000 through 20043). This directly contradicts the original text, which implies that California law would take precedence in case of inconsistencies. The contradicted text is within the same section, emphasizing the contradiction. This is a structural flaw that leads to an in-text contradiction as the clause is essentially placed in multiple locations (original and changed) with conflicting implications about which controls.

perturbation.original_text: 8. You must sign a general release if you renew or transfer your franchise. California Corporation Code 31512 voids a waiver of your rights under the Franchise Investment Law (California Corporations Code 31000 through 31516). Business and Professions Code 20010 voids a waiver of your rights under the Franchise Relations Act (Business and Professions Code 20000 through 20043).
perturbation.changed_text: 8. You must sign a general release if you renew or transfer your franchise. This release constitutes a full and irrevocable waiver of all rights, including those under the Franchise Investment Law (California Corporations Code 31000 through 31516) and the Franchise Relations Act (Business and Professions Code 20000 through 20043), to the fullest extent permitted by law. The release will be upheld regardless of existing legislation.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the general release constitutes a 'full and irrevocable waiver of all rights,' the modified clause directly contradicts the subsequent mention that specific California codes void waivers of rights under franchise laws. This creates an in-text contradiction by asserting the validity of a waiver that the very next sentences suggest is invalid under California law. Thus introducing in-text contradiction due to the addition of the new clauses to the text.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8. You must sign a general release if you renew or transfer your franchise. This release constitutes a full and irrevocable waiver of all rights, including those under the Franchise Investment Law (California Corporations Code 31000 through 31516) and the Franchise Relations Act (Business and Professions Code 20000 through 20043), to the fullest extent permitted by law. The release will be upheld regardless of existing legislation.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by stating that the general release constitutes a 'full and irrevocable waiver of all rights,' even those rights that the original text acknowledges are protected under California Corporation Code 31512 and Business and Professions Code 20010. The phrase 'This release will be upheld regardless of existing legislation' directly clashes with the understanding that certain waivers are void under California law, creating a strong in-text contradiction.
